questions.title: ROS2 Exercise: Classes, Attributes and Instance Methods
questions.context: Create a RobotManager class that logs robot specifications. Split the code into a module file robot_manager.py containing the class and a separate node file robot_manager_node.py containing the main entry point. Use rclpy for ROS2 integration.

parts.label: Part I
tasks: Create ROS2 Python package 'my_robot_manager' with dependency rclpy
Create robot_manager.py inside the my_robot_manager/ module directory
Define class RobotManager with instance attributes: robot_name (str), robot_model (str), battery_capacity_kwh (float), total_operation_hours (int)
Set default values in __init__: battery_capacity_kwh = 0.16, total_operation_hours = 2560
Write method print_specifications() that logs all attributes using self.get_logger().info() — inherit from rclpy Node or accept a logger parameter
Update setup.py to include the module

parts.label: Part II
tasks: Create robot_manager_node.py in my_robot_manager/
Import RobotManager class
Initialize rclpy and create a ROS2 node named 'robot_manager'
Instantiate one RobotManager object and assign: robot_name = 'my_robot', robot_model = 'TurtleBot3'
Call print_specifications()
Add entry point 'robot_manager_node' in setup.py pointing to robot_manager_node:main

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Robot Name: my_robot
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Robot Model: TurtleBot3
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Battery Capacity (kWh): 0.16
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Total Operation Hours: 2560

questions.title: ROS2 Exercise: Using 'self' to access private attributes
questions.context: Modify the RobotManager class from the previous exercise. Instead of printing [X,Y] in odom_callback(), create a ROS2 Trigger service that logs the current robot position when called. Use self to access private attributes current_x_position and current_y_position.
prerequisites: unit_3_ex_3.4 — my_robot_manager package with RobotManager class
questions.notes: Open the existing files: robot_manager.py and robot_manager_node.py inside the my_robot_manager package.
tasks: Declare two new private attributes: self._current_x_position (float) and self._current_y_position (float), initialized to 0.0
In odom_callback(), assign msg.pose.pose.position.x to self._current_x_position and y similarly
Remove any get_logger().info() call that logs position from odom_callback()
Create a Trigger service whose name is self.robot_name + '/log_current_position'
In the trigger callback, log self._current_x_position and self._current_y_position
Set response.success = True and response.message = 'Position logged'
Import Trigger from std_srvs.srv and add std_srvs to package dependencies
Update package.xml and setup.py with std_srvs dependency

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Robot 1 created.
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Robot Name: Turty1
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Robot Model: TurtleBot3
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Battery Capacity (kWh): 0.16
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_manager]: Total Operation Hours: 2560

questions.title: Define a ComputerUnit class to read total CPU load and total memory usage
questions.context: Create a ComputerUnit class that reads system performance metrics (CPU load, memory usage) using an abstract ISystemInformation interface and a SystemInformation implementation. This class will later be embedded inside a RobotManagerComposition class via composition. Code for ISystemInformation and SystemInformation is provided.
questions.notes: Create ROS2 Python package robot_manager_composition with dependencies rclpy and std_srvs. The ISystemInformation interface and SystemInformation implementation code is provided — copy it into system_information.py.
tasks: Create ROS2 Python package 'robot_manager_composition' with dependencies rclpy and std_srvs
Create system_information.py in robot_manager_composition/ using the provided code
Create system_information_test_node.py in robot_manager_composition/ with a main() that instantiates ComputerUnit and calls print_info()
Add entry point 'system_information_test' in setup.py pointing to system_information_test_node:main

expected_output.output:
Total CPU usage: <value>%
Total memory usage: <value>%

questions.title: RobotManagerComposition class with console output service
questions.context: Create a RobotManagerComposition class inside the robot_manager_composition package. It starts a ROS2 SetBool service called 'robot_manager_output' that enables or disables console output via the output_enabled boolean flag.
tasks: Create robot_manager_composition.py in robot_manager_composition/
Define class RobotManagerComposition(Node) with protected attribute self._output_enabled = False
Add a ROS2 service server and method _init_config_output_srv()
Advertise service named 'robot_manager_output' of type std_srvs/srv/SetBool
In callback _config_output_callback: set self._output_enabled from request.data, return appropriate response message ('Console output enabled.' or 'Console output disabled.')
Create robot_manager_composition_node.py in robot_manager_composition/ with main()
Add entry point 'robot_manager_composition_node' in setup.py

questions.title: Inheritance: Create RobotManagerBase base class
questions.context: Create a new Robot Manager program using inheritance. RobotManagerBase is the base class for all robot types. RobotManagerMobile is the derived class for mobile bases. Start with the base class.
questions.notes: Create ROS2 Python package robot_manager_inheritance with dependencies rclpy, nav_msgs, std_srvs.
tasks: Create ROS2 Python package 'robot_manager_inheritance' with dependencies rclpy, nav_msgs, std_srvs
Create base_robot_manager.py in robot_manager_inheritance/
Define class RobotManagerBase(Node) with protected: self._output_enabled = False, method _init_config_output_srv()
In _init_config_output_srv(): advertise service 'robot_manager_output' of type std_srvs/srv/SetBool
Implement _config_output_callback: enable/disable self._output_enabled, return appropriate response messages
Create base_robot_manager_node.py in robot_manager_inheritance/ with main() for testing the base class
Add entry point 'robot_manager_base_class_test_node' in setup.py

files_to_create.role: test entry point for base class

questions.title: Inheritance: Create RobotManagerMobile derived class
questions.context: Create a minimal derived class RobotManagerMobile that inherits from RobotManagerBase and calls the parent class methods to initialize the robot_manager_output service and an odometry subscriber.
tasks: Create mobile_robot_manager.py in robot_manager_inheritance/
Define class MobileRobotManager(RobotManagerBase) importing from base_robot_manager
In __init__: call super().__init__(node_name), then call self._init_config_output_srv()
Subscribe to /odom topic of type nav_msgs/msg/Odometry
Create mobile_robot_manager_node.py in robot_manager_inheritance/ with main() function
Add entry point 'mobile_robot_manager_node' in setup.py

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [mobile_robot_manager]: Enable Output Service created
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [mobile_robot_manager]: Odometry subscriber created

questions.title: Generic ROS2 topic subscriber for Image using callable patterns
questions.context: Create a generic subscriber pattern in Python for subscribing to a ROS2 topic. Build two nodes: one using standalone generic functions (topic_subscriber_function_node.py) and one using a generic subscriber class (topic_subscriber_class_node.py). Both subscribe to /camera/rgb/image_raw of type sensor_msgs/Image and print msg.data[0].
questions.notes: Hint: Start by creating a basic ROS2 topic subscriber WITHOUT generics first. Once working, add the generic/callable abstraction.
tasks: Create topic_subscriber_function_node.py: use a generic callback function that accepts any message type and a topic string; subscribe to /camera/rgb/image_raw
Create topic_subscriber_class_node.py: define a GenericSubscriber class that takes a message type and topic as constructor parameters
Both nodes: callback must print the first element of msg.data using msg.data[0]
topic_subscriber_function_node.py: generic behavior is achieved via a parameterized function, no class-level type parameter
topic_subscriber_class_node.py: the class stores the message type and uses it in create_subscription()
Add both entry points in setup.py

questions.title: Reuse generic subscriber for PointCloud2
questions.context: Extend the work from Exercise 6.1 by creating a new node pcl_topic_subscriber_node.py that reuses the same generic subscriber implementation from the Image subscriber to subscribe to a PointCloud2 topic.
prerequisites: unit_6_ex_6.1 — unit6_exercises package with generic subscriber implementation
tasks: Create pcl_topic_subscriber_node.py in unit6_exercises/
Import and reuse the GenericSubscriber class from Exercise 6.1
Subscribe to /camera/depth_registered/points of type sensor_msgs/msg/PointCloud2
Instantiate GenericSubscriber passing PointCloud2 as the message type
Use msg.data[0] in the callback same as for Image
Add entry point 'pcl_topic_subscriber_node' in setup.py

questions.title: Final Exercise: Type-dispatched callbacks with edge detection and depth image
questions.context: Add type-specific processing to the generic subscriber callbacks for Image and PointCloud2 types. For Image: perform Canny edge detection and publish to /out/edge_detection. For PointCloud2: convert to depth image and publish to /out/depth_image. For any other type: print the message.
prerequisites: unit_6_ex_6.2 — unit6_exercises with generic subscriber class
questions.notes: Recommended: First implement edge detection and PointCloud-to-depth-image as standalone scripts to test independently, then integrate into the type-dispatch pattern.
tasks: Create specialised_complete.py and specialised_complete_node.py in unit6_exercises/
For Image callback (detected via isinstance or message type): convert to grayscale, apply GaussianBlur, run Canny edge detection, publish result to /out/edge_detection using cv_bridge
For PointCloud2 callback: convert pointcloud to depth image using sensor_msgs_py or open3d, publish to /out/depth_image
For any other message type: just log the message
Node name: specialised_complete_node
Update package.xml and setup.py: add cv_bridge, sensor_msgs_py, std_msgs dependencies and entry point

questions.title: Basic Python Thread Creation
questions.context: Create a simple Python program using the threading module. Launch a thread that executes a function and ensure the main thread waits for it to finish using join().
tasks: Create a new directory multithreading_exercises in your home directory
Create exercise1.py inside it
Import the threading module
Create a function that the thread will execute — it should print a message
Create a threading.Thread object with target set to the function
Call thread.start() to launch the thread
Call thread.join() to wait for the thread to finish before main() exits
Run with: python3 exercise1.py

questions.title: Validate robot turn velocity input using try/except/raise
questions.context: Use the robot_commander library to steer a simulated robot based on user input. Validate that the turn velocity is a decimal number between -1.0 and 0.0. Use try/except/raise to handle invalid inputs gracefully.
prerequisites: robot_commander library — clone from theconstructcore/modern_cpp_course (Python port)
questions.notes: Inside ~/ros2_ws/src/exercises_unit_8/exercises_unit_8/ create validate_user_input.py. Paste the starter code and read it before writing new code.
tasks: Include exception handling using try, except and raise inside the while loop
Read turn_velocity using: turn_velocity_str = input('Enter turn velocity: ')
Try to convert input to float; if it fails (ValueError), raise an exception for non-decimal input
If turn_velocity is between -1.0 and 0.0: call my_robot.turn(turn_velocity, 5)
Else: raise a ValueError for out-of-range value
Add an except ValueError block: print 'Enter turn velocity between -1.0 and 0.0'
Add a broad except Exception block: print 'Please enter a decimal number.'
Update package.xml and setup.py: add robot_commander dependency and entry point

files_to_create.role: main entry point with exception handling

starter_code.content:
import rclpy
from robot_commander.robot_commander import RobotCommander

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    my_robot = RobotCommander()
    n = 0
    while n < 5:
        # INSERT YOUR CODE BELOW THIS LINE

        # INSERT YOUR CODE ABOVE THIS LINE
        n += 1
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Initializing node
Enter turn velocity: 5
Enter turn velocity between -1.0 and 0.0
Enter turn velocity: -6
Enter turn velocity between -1.0 and 0.0
Enter turn velocity: -0.5
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Turning clockwise
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Stopping the robot
Enter turn velocity: g
Please enter a decimal number.

questions.title: Custom exception class WrongTurnException
questions.context: Continue from Exercise 8.1. Replace the generic ValueError with a custom exception class WrongTurnException that inherits from Exception and overrides __str__() to return a descriptive error message.
questions.notes: Open ~/ros2_ws/src/exercises_unit_8/exercises_unit_8/validate_user_input.py for editing.
tasks: Define class WrongTurnException(Exception) in validate_user_input.py
Override __str__() to return: 'Allowed turn velocity only between -1.0 and 0.0'
Modify the try/except block to raise WrongTurnException() instead of ValueError for out-of-range values
Add a specific except WrongTurnException as e block that calls str(e) or print(e)
Keep the broad except Exception block for non-decimal input errors

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Initializing node
Enter turn velocity: 5
Allowed turn velocity only between -1.0 and 0.0
Enter turn velocity: -6
Allowed turn velocity only between -1.0 and 0.0
Enter turn velocity: -0.5
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Turning clockwise
[INFO] [<timestamp>] [robot_commander]: Stopping the robot
Enter turn velocity: g
Please enter a decimal number.

questions.title: ROS2 Exercise: Custom Service Message with Duration Parameter
questions.context: Modify the Python scripts to include a custom service message. The BB-8 robot service now accepts a duration parameter that specifies how long the robot should execute movement commands.
questions.notes: Modify existing Python class to pass duration parameter to service message and update service server code accordingly.
tasks: Define a custom service message with int32 duration field
Modify the Python class so duration is passed to the service message
Update the Service Server code to accept and use the specified duration
Test that the BB-8 robot executes movement for the specified duration

questions.title: ROS2 Services Quiz: BB-8 Square Movement Service
questions.context: Create a ROS2 Service Server that commands BB-8 to move in a square pattern. The service accepts side length and number of repetitions as parameters.
questions.notes: Package name: services_quiz. Service name: /move_bb8_in_square_custom. Service message type: BB8CustomServiceMessage.
tasks: Create services_quiz ROS2 package
Define BB8CustomServiceMessage.srv with side (float64) and repetitions (int32) fields
Create service server that accepts side and repetitions parameters
Implement square movement logic
Repeat the square movement based on repetitions parameter
Create corresponding service client to test the server
Make sure service name is /move_bb8_in_square_custom

questions.title: ROS2 Topics Quiz: Obstacle Avoidance with Laser
questions.context: Create a ROS2 node that subscribes to laser scan data and publishes velocity commands to avoid obstacles. The robot should navigate forward and turn when detecting obstacles closer than 1 meter.
questions.notes: Package: topics_quiz. Node: topics_quiz_node. Subscribe to /kobuki/laser/scan. Publish to /cmd_vel. Logic: if front < 1m → turn left; if right < 1m → turn left; if left < 1m → turn right; else → move forward.
tasks: Create topics_quiz ROS2 package with rclpy dependency
Create topics_quiz_node that subscribes to /kobuki/laser/scan topic
Extract ranges array from LaserScan message
Implement obstacle avoidance logic based on laser ranges
If front reading < 1m: publish turn left command to /cmd_vel
If right reading < 1m: publish turn left command
If left reading < 1m: publish turn right command
Else: publish forward movement command
Test the node with simulated or real laser data

questions.title: ROS2 Actions Quiz: Drone Takeoff and Land
questions.context: Create a ROS2 Action Server that controls drone takeoff and landing. The action server receives goals (TAKEOFF or LAND commands), executes them, provides feedback on progress, and returns results on success or failure.
questions.notes: Package: actions_quiz. Action: /action_custom_msg_as. Goal: string (TAKEOFF or LAND). Feedback: string (current action status). Result: bool (success flag).
tasks: Create actions_quiz ROS2 package
Define CustomActionMsg.action with string goal, string feedback, bool success result
Create action server that accepts TAKEOFF or LAND goals
Implement takeoff: transition drone from landed to flying state
Implement land: transition drone from flying to landed state
Publish feedback during action execution showing current status
Return result with success = true when action completes
Create action client to test the server with both TAKEOFF and LAND goals

questions.title: Probability Exercise 4.2: Distribution Analysis
questions.context: Analyze a probability distribution for robot position predictions. Given possible positions and their probabilities, calculate expected value, variance, and standard deviation.
questions.notes: Use numpy functions: np.dot, np.multiply, np.sqrt. Use utilities.plot_distro to visualize. File location: ~/catkin_ws/src/mathsrobotics/unit4_exercises/unit4_exercise2.py
tasks: Plot the distribution using plot_distro(x, p, 'normal')
Calculate expected value E[X] = sum(x * p(x))
Calculate E[X^2] = sum(x^2 * p(x))
Calculate variance Var = E[X^2] - E[X]^2
Calculate standard deviation STD = sqrt(Var)
Print all calculated values

questions.title: Calculus Exercise 3.3: Robot Velocity and Position Integration
questions.context: Work with TurtleBot kinematics by implementing velocity functions to move the robot. Choose between constant velocity, linear velocity, or quadratic velocity functions and observe how position changes over time.
questions.notes: File: move_velocity.py in ~/catkin_ws/src/mathsrobotics/move_robot/src/. Implement one of three velocity functions: v(t)=0, v(t)=1, or v(t)=0.002t. Publish to /cmd_vel topic.
tasks: Import rospy, Twist, Odometry from nav_msgs
Create TurtlebotMoves class with odometry subscriber and velocity publisher
Implement odometryCallback to extract x position from /odom topic
Implement move() function with one of three velocity options
Choose velocity function: constant (0), linear (1), or quadratic (0.002*t)
Create Twist message and publish velocity command to /cmd_vel
Observe robot motion and position output

expected_output.output:
X position of the robot: 0.123456
X position of the robot: 0.234567
X position of the robot: 0.345678

questions.title: Probability Exercise 4.3: Bayesian Belief Update
questions.context: Apply Bayes' rule to update robot beliefs about door state. Given an action (closing a door) and prior belief, calculate posterior belief using conditional probabilities.
questions.notes: Robot in front of door. Action: close door. Prior: P(open) = 0.67. Calculate P(open|action) and P(closed|action) using Bayes rule and given conditional probabilities.
tasks: Given P(open) = 0.67 (prior probability door is open)
Given P(closed|open, action) = 0.9 (action succeeds 90% of time when door open)
Given P(open|open, action) = 0.1 (action fails 10% of time when door open)
Given P(closed|closed, action) = 1.0 (action always succeeds when door already closed)
Given P(open|closed, action) = 0.0 (action never changes closed door to open)
Apply Bayes rule: P(state|action) = sum(P(state|action,x) * P(x))
Calculate posterior P(open|action) and P(closed|action)
Print belief values

questions.title: ROS2 Actions Exercise 9.12: Fibonacci Action Server
questions.context: Create a Fibonacci action server that receives an order parameter and computes Fibonacci sequence. The server publishes feedback with current sequence elements and returns the complete sequence as the result.
questions.notes: Use actionlib_tutorials Fibonacci action message. Server must handle goal execution and publish result when done.
tasks: Launch Fibonacci action server using provided code from actionlib_tutorials
Test the server by calling it with different Fibonacci order values
Echo the result topic to see published results
Echo the feedback topic to see feedback during computation
Call the action server: rostopic pub /fibonacci_as/goal actionlib_tutorials/FibonacciActionGoal 'header: {}, goal_id: {}, goal: {order: 10}'
Verify sequence is computed correctly and returned

questions.title: Probability Exercise 4.1: Maze Door Probability
questions.context: A robot escapes a maze by randomly choosing left or right at each crossroad. Given conditional probabilities at each decision point, calculate the probability of exiting at each of 8 doors.
questions.notes: Robot must choose: left (50%), right (50%). Compute probability of reaching each door using conditional probability logic.
tasks: Create probability maze diagram with 8 possible exit doors
At each crossroad, robot has 50% chance to go left, 50% to go right
Calculate conditional probabilities: P(Door i) = P(left|left)*P(left|left)*P(left) etc.
For Doors 4-8: calculate similarly
Print all door probabilities
Verify most likely door is Door 5 with P=0.25

questions.title: TF2 Exercise 1.5: Create a Coordinate Frame
questions.context: Learn to create and publish coordinate frames using ROS2 TF2. Use the static_transform_publisher to define spatial relationships between parent and child frames.
questions.notes: Use tf2_ros package. Command structure: ros2 run tf2_ros static_transform_publisher x y z yaw pitch roll frame_id child_frame_id
tasks: Launch static_transform_publisher node
Define relationship between parent frame (map) and child frame (robot_base)
Provide 6DOF transformation: x, y, z (position) and yaw, pitch, roll (rotation)
Verify frame is published using ros2 tf2_echo command
Check frame tree using ros2 run tf2_tools view_frames

expected_output.output: [INFO] [<timestamp>]: Spinning ROS node, listening for transforms. Press Ctrl+C to exit.

run_commands: ros2 run tf2_ros static_transform_publisher 1 0 0 0 0 0 map robot_base

questions.title: URDF Exercise 6.1: Add RGB Camera and PointCloud Sensors
questions.context: Extend a robot URDF model by adding two sensors: an RGB wide-angle camera and a PointCloud camera (LiDAR). Configure both sensors in Gazebo simulation with appropriate parameters.
questions.notes: Modify box_bot URDF. Add two sensor links and joints. Configure camera properties: resolution, FOV, format. Configure LiDAR: scan samples, angle range, update rate.
tasks: Add RGB wide-angle camera sensor to robot model
Configure camera: horizontal FOV 6.283, image size 320x240, format R8G8B8
Add PointCloud (LiDAR) sensor to robot model
Configure LiDAR: 50 horizontal samples, 50 vertical samples, scan range min/max
Create camera link and joint connected to chassis
Create pointcloud link and joint connected to chassis
Set publish rate to 30Hz for both sensors
Create launch file: spawn_robot_ros2_final.launch.xml
Create visualization launch: urdf_visualize_final.launch.py

expected_output.output: [INFO] [<timestamp>]: Gazebo launching robot model with sensors

questions.title: Bayes Filter Exercise 2.10: Predict Step with Convolution
questions.context: Implement the predict step of a Bayes Filter for robot localization. Use convolution to model how movement noise spreads the robot's position belief across the map.
questions.notes: Implement predict_step() function that convolves a kernel (movement noise model) with the belief distribution. File: bayes_filter.py in bayes_tutorials package.
tasks: Open bayes_filter.py in bayes_tutorials package
Locate movement_callback() function
Implement predict_step(belief, offset, kernel) function
Shift kernel by offset to model movement distance
Apply convolution: elementwise multiply kernel with belief, sum results
For each position: output[i] += belief[index] * kernel[k]
Call predict_step in movement_callback after measurement
Launch bayes_filter node and visualize belief distribution
Call measurement update and predict step services

expected_output.output: [INFO] [<timestamp>]: Bayes Filter predict step applied. Belief updated with movement noise.

questions.title: Nav2 Exercise 3.3: Set Robot Initial Pose
questions.context: Set the robot's initial pose for navigation and localization. Use RVIZ to identify coordinates and publish them to the /initialpose topic so the localization system knows where the robot starts.
questions.notes: Use ros2 topic pub command to publish to /initialpose. Topic type: geometry_msgs/msg/PoseWithCovariance. Get coordinates from RVIZ Publish Point tool.
tasks: Open RVIZ and view the map
Use 'Publish Point' tool to click a location on the map
Note the coordinates displayed in the terminal
Use ros2 topic pub to publish initial pose to /initialpose
Include position x, y coordinates and orientation quaternion
Run command: ros2 topic pub -1 /initialpose geometry_msgs/msg/PoseWithCovariance '{header: ...}'
run_commands: ros2 topic pub -l /initialpose geometry_msgs/msg/PoseWithCovariance "{header: {}, pose: {position: {x: 1.0, y: 2.0}}, covariance: [0.0]}"

questions.title: Nav3 Exercise 4.3: Send Navigation Goal via Topic
questions.context: Send a navigation goal to the robot using the /goal_pose topic. The robot will compute a path and navigate to the specified location on the map.
questions.notes: Use ros2 topic pub to send goal. Goal type: geometry_msgs/msg/PoseStamped. Use RVIZ Publish Point to get coordinates.
tasks: Get goal position from RVIZ Publish Point tool
Click on a target location in RVIZ map view
Note the coordinates displayed
Publish goal to /goal_pose topic using ros2 topic pub
Include position (x, y, z) and orientation (quaternion)
Robot will start moving toward the goal
run_commands: ros2 topic pub -l /goal_pose geometry_msgs/msg/PoseStamped "{header: {frame_id: 'map'}, pose: {position: {x: 5.0, y: 3.0}}}"

questions.title: Nav4 Exercise 5.5: Create Single Navigation Launch File
questions.context: Consolidate multiple navigation nodes (map_server, localization, planner, controller, recovery_server) into a single launch file managed by a nav2_lifecycle_manager for cleaner robot startup.
questions.notes: File: navigation.launch.py in pathplanner_server package. Include: map_server, amcl, controller, planner, recovery_server, lifecycle_manager. Use lifecycle_manager to manage all nodes.
tasks: Create navigation.launch.py in pathplanner_server package
Import LaunchDescription, Node from launch packages
Define nodes for: nav2_map_server, nav2_amcl, nav2_controller, nav2_planner, nav2_recoveries_server
Create nav2_lifecycle_manager node to manage all navigation nodes
Configure lifecycle_manager with generic name 'lifecycle_manager'
Include config files for each node from package share directories
Return LaunchDescription with all nodes
Test launch file: ros2 launch pathplanner_server navigation.launch.py

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [launch]: All specified launch arguments are in use
[INFO] [nav2_map_server]: Map server started
[INFO] [nav2_amcl]: AMCL started
[INFO] [lifecycle_manager]: Lifecycle manager started

questions.title: Final Project: Autonomous Maze Navigation
questions.context: Program a simulated TurtleBot to navigate and escape a maze autonomously. The robot randomly chooses to go left or right at each intersection using probability-based decision making.
questions.notes: Modify TurtlebotProject class to implement decide_left_or_right() function. Use numpy.random.uniform for probability. Robot starts at top of maze, oriented right. Goal: exit at bottom.
tasks: Create decide_left_or_right(limit=0.8) function in TurtlebotProject class
Use np.random.uniform(0, 1) to generate random number
If random < limit: return True (go right, with 80% probability)
Else: return False (go left, with 20% probability)
Integrate function into movement logic for maze navigation
Robot must navigate from start (top, facing right) to exit (bottom)
Test behavior: robot should randomly explore maze
Verify: robot reaches exit with different random paths on each run

expected_output.output:
Robot entering maze
Random decision: go right (0.623 < 0.8)
Robot at intersection
Random decision: go left (0.921 > 0.8)
Robot reached exit!

questions.title: Intermediate ROS2 Final Project: Circle Detection and Navigation
questions.context: Create a ROS2 system that detects circular shapes using laser scan data, extracts their positions and radii, and commands the robot to move toward them. This capstone project integrates sensing, data processing, and robot control.
questions.notes: Package: ros2_intermediate_project. Use LaserDataProcess class to detect circles. Use sklearn clustering and rclpy library for circle detection from laser scans.
tasks: Create ros2_intermediate_project ROS2 package
Create LaserDataProcess class with circle detection capability
Subscribe to /scan topic (sensor_msgs/msg/LaserScan)
Convert laser scan ranges to X-Y Cartesian coordinates
Use sklearn AgglomerativeClustering for grouping nearby points
Use sklearn neighbors.kneighbors_graph for connectivity
Detect circular patterns in clustered data
Extract circle center (X, Y) and radius from clustered points
Publish detected circles to output topic
Create navigation node to move robot toward detected circles
Publish velocity commands to /cmd_vel based on circle position

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [circle_navigator]: Detected circle at X=1.2, Y=2.3, radius=0.15
[INFO] [circle_navigator]: Moving toward circle
[INFO] [circle_navigator]: Reached circle position

questions.title: Behavior Trees Course Project: Multi-Cell Robot Navigation
questions.context: Design and implement a behavior tree for a robot to autonomously navigate between cells. The robot must use a laser to detect freeways, open doors when needed, and traverse multiple cells using branching behavior tree logic.
questions.notes: Environment: 3-cell world with doorways. Robot starts in cell 1, must reach cell 3. Doors can be manually opened. Use behavior tree framework (py_trees or BT++) to define robot logic.
tasks: Create behavior tree ROS2 package
Define behavior tree structure: Root → Selector → Sequences → Actions
Implement condition nodes: is_door_open, is_wall_ahead, is_goal_reached
Implement action nodes: scan_environment, open_door, move_forward, turn
Robot must transition from cell 1 to cell 2: detect freeway, navigate
At doorway: check if door open; if closed, wait until open
Robot must transition from cell 2 to cell 3: same logic
Use laser scan feedback for navigation decisions
Implement tree execution in ROS2 node
Test behavior tree with manual door opening via terminal commands

expected_output.output:
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Robot in Cell 1
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Scanning for freeway
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Freeway detected
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Moving to Cell 2
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Door detected
[INFO] [bt_executor]: Waiting for door to open...
